43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94

97	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}

99	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
100	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
101	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
102	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
103	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
104	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
105	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
106	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
107	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
108	  % \varphi$."
109	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
110	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
111	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
112	  \[
113	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
114	  \]
115	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
116	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
117	  \[
118	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
119	  \]
120	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).

130	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
131	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.

192	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
193	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
194	
195	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
196	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
197	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
198	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
199	
200	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
201	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
202	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
203	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
204

297	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
299	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_standardSmooth}
300	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
301	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
302	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
303	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
304	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
305	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
306	  \(S_M\)-module.
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  The canonical comparison map
311	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
312	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
313	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
314	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
315	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
316	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
317	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
318	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
319	  the conclusion.
320	\end{proof}

342	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}

383	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
384	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
385	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
386	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
387	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
388	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
389	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
390	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
391	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
392	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
393	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
394	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
395	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
396	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
397	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
398	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
399	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
400	  \[
401	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
402	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
403	  \]
404	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
405	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
406	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
407	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
408	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
409	
410	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
411	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
412	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
413	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
414	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
415	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
416	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
417	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
418	\end{lemma}
419

421	% we know that $r = \text{trdeg}_k\ \kappa(\mathfrak p)$ is equal to the
422	% dimension of $V(\mathfrak p)$. Pick any maximal chain of primes
423	% $\mathfrak p \subset \mathfrak p_1 \subset \ldots \subset \mathfrak p_r$
424	% starting with $\mathfrak p$ in $S$. This has length $r$ by Lemma
425	% \ref{lemma-dimension-spell-it-out}. Let $\mathfrak q_j$, $j \in J$ be
426	% the minimal primes of $S$ which are contained in $\mathfrak p$. These
427	% correspond $1-1$ to minimal primes in $S_{\mathfrak p}$ via the rule
428	% $\mathfrak q_j \mapsto \mathfrak q_jS_{\mathfrak p}$. By Lemma
429	% \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-over-field} we know that
430	% $\dim_x(X)$ is equal to the maximum of the dimensions of the rings
431	% $S/\mathfrak q_j$. For each $j$ pick a maximal chain of primes
432	% $\mathfrak q_j \subset \mathfrak p'_1 \subset \ldots \subset
433	% \mathfrak p'_{s(j)} = \mathfrak p$. Then $\dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) =
434	% \max_{j \in J} s(j)$. Now, each chain
435	% $\mathfrak q_j \subset \mathfrak p'_1 \subset \ldots \subset
436	% \mathfrak p'_{s(j)} = \mathfrak p \subset \mathfrak p_1 \subset \ldots
437	% \subset \mathfrak p_r$ is a maximal chain in $S/\mathfrak q_j$, and by
438	% what was said before we have $\dim_x(X) = \max_{j \in J} r + s(j)$.
439	% The lemma follows."
440	\begin{proof}
441	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
442	        lem:ringKrullDim_localization_atMaximal_mvPolynomial,
443	        lem:ringKrullDim_quotient_localization_mvPolynomial_regular,
444	        lem:matsumura_isRegular_of_linearIndependent_cotangent,
445	        lem:exists_submersivePresentation_of_standardSmoothReldim,
446	        lem:submersive_relation_cotangent_linearIndependent_localized}
447	  This is exactly Stacks tag~\texttt{00OE}
448	  (\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}); the proof is a
449	  citation of the dimension theory of finitely generated algebras over a
450	  field. By Noether normalisation the finite-type \(k\)-algebra \(S\) is
451	  finite over a polynomial subalgebra, so the going-up / catenary
452	  dimension theory of such algebras applies. Write
453	  \(r = \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); this transcendence
454	  degree is exactly the dimension of the closed subvariety
455	  \(\overline{\{x\}} = V(\mathfrak q)\). A maximal chain of primes
456	  through \(\mathfrak q\) therefore splits into a
457	  length-\(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) part below \(\mathfrak q\) (recording
458	  the local codimension, i.e.\ the height of \(\mathfrak q\) in the
459	  relevant irreducible component) and a length-\(r\) part above it
460	  (recording the dimension of \(\overline{\{x\}}\)). Taking the maximum
461	  over the minimal primes contained in \(\mathfrak q\) yields
462	  \[
463	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + r
464	    = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q),
465	  \]

468	  For the geometric specialisation matching the Lean target, take
469	  \(k = \bar k\) algebraically closed and \(S\) standard smooth of
470	  relative dimension \(n\), so that \(\dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S) = n\) via
471	  \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (the free
472	  K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}\) has rank \(n\), which
473	  pins the relative dimension). The formula then gives

475	  q)\). At a closed point \(\mathfrak q\) is maximal, so by the
476	  Nullstellensatz \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\) and
477	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\); hence
478	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S) = n\).
479	\end{proof}

512	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
513	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
514	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
515	  \[
516	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
517	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
518	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
519	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
520	  \]
521	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
522	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
523	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
524	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}

528	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
529	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),

532	  \]
533	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
534	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
535	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
536	\end{lemma}
